tasks.prompt: Warm-up: Write a Pod YAML named `preflight-pod` that has a single init container named `check-config` using the `busybox:1.36` image. The init container should print the message `config ready` and exit successfully. The main application container should be named `app` using `nginx:1.27` and expose port 80. Confirm that the `initContainers` field is at the same level as `containers` inside `spec`.
tasks.starter:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: preflight-pod
spec:
  # TODO: add initContainers block here
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx:1.27
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
tasks.solution:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: preflight-pod
spec:
  initContainers:
    - name: check-config
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ['sh', '-c', 'echo config ready']
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx:1.27
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
tasks.explanation: The `initContainers` field lives inside `spec`, at the same indentation level as `containers`. Kubernetes runs every entry under `initContainers` in declaration order, one at a time, waiting for each to exit with code 0 before moving to the next. Only after all init containers succeed does Kubernetes start the containers listed under `containers`. Here there is only one init container; it executes `echo config ready` (exit code 0 automatically) and terminates, after which `nginx` is allowed to start. Note that init containers do not take `livenessProbe` or `readinessProbe` â€” they are expected to run once and finish, so probes are not applicable.
hints: The `initContainers` key belongs under `spec:`, not under `containers:` or at the top level of the manifest.
A shell command that only prints and does nothing else exits with code 0. Use `command: ['sh', '-c', 'echo config ready']` to run a shell one-liner.

Apply the manifest with `kubectl apply -f preflight-pod.yaml` and then watch with `kubectl get pod preflight-pod -w`. You should see the status move through `Init:0/1` â†’ `PodInitializing` â†’ `Running`.

tasks.prompt:
Core practice 1 â€” Dependency wait with two sequential init containers: Write a Pod YAML named `app-with-deps` that models a real dependency-wait pattern. Add two init containers in this exact order:
1. `wait-for-db` (image `busybox:1.36`): loops every 2 seconds running `nslookup mydb` until it succeeds, then exits.
2. `wait-for-cache` (image `busybox:1.36`): loops every 2 seconds running `nslookup mycache` until it succeeds, then exits.
After both succeed, start the main container `backend` (image `nginx:1.27`, port 80).
The ordering guarantee is critical: `wait-for-cache` must not start until `wait-for-db` has exited successfully.
tasks.starter:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-deps
spec:
  initContainers:
    # TODO: add wait-for-db init container
    # TODO: add wait-for-cache init container
  containers:
    - name: backend
      image: nginx:1.27
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
tasks.solution:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-deps
spec:
  initContainers:
    - name: wait-for-db
      image: busybox:1.36
      command:
        - sh
        - -c
        - "until nslookup mydb; do echo 'waiting for mydb'; sleep 2; done"
    - name: wait-for-cache
      image: busybox:1.36
      command:
        - sh
        - -c
        - "until nslookup mycache; do echo 'waiting for mycache'; sleep 2; done"
  containers:
    - name: backend
      image: nginx:1.27
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
tasks.explanation: Kubernetes runs init containers strictly in the order they appear in the YAML list. `wait-for-db` starts first; because it loops until `nslookup mydb` succeeds (exit 0), it blocks indefinitely if the DB is not reachable. Only when it exits cleanly does `wait-for-cache` start, applying the same blocking loop for `mycache`. Only when both have exited with code 0 does Kubernetes start `backend`. This is the dependency-wait pattern: the Pod stays in `Init:0/2` â†’ `Init:1/2` â†’ `Running` and never crash-loops the app container. In a real cluster you would create Services named `mydb` and `mycache`; once those Services exist, `nslookup` resolves them and the init containers unblock. Using `until â€¦ done` rather than a one-shot check is the safe form because DNS propagation may take a few seconds after the Service is created.
hints: List `wait-for-db` first and `wait-for-cache` second in `initContainers`. The list position is the execution order â€” Kubernetes does not sort or parallelize them.

The shell loop pattern is: `until <command>; do echo 'waiting'; sleep 2; done`. When `<command>` exits 0 the `until` condition becomes false and the shell exits 0, satisfying Kubernetes's success requirement.

To test without a real cluster, apply the manifest to a local kind or minikube cluster that has no `mydb` Service. Run `kubectl get pod app-with-deps` and observe it stuck at `Init:0/2`. Then create a dummy Service (`kubectl create service clusterip mydb --tcp=5432:5432`) and watch it advance to `Init:1/2`, confirming the ordering.

tasks.prompt:
Core practice 2 â€” Native sidecar with a shared volume: Write a Pod YAML named `native-sidecar-pod` that demonstrates the Kubernetes 1.28+ native sidecar pattern. Requirements:
- Declare an `emptyDir` volume named `shared-logs`.
- In `initContainers`, declare a sidecar container named `log-agent` (image `busybox:1.36`) with `restartPolicy: Always`. It should run `tail -F /var/log/app/app.log` and mount `shared-logs` at `/var/log/app`.
- In `containers`, declare the main app named `myapp` (image `busybox:1.36`). It should write `$(date) - request handled` to `/var/log/app/app.log` every 3 seconds in an infinite loop, and mount the same `shared-logs` volume at `/var/log/app`.
Explain in a comment inside the YAML why `restartPolicy: Always` on an init container is what makes it a native sidecar.
tasks.starter:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: native-sidecar-pod
spec:
  initContainers:
    # TODO: declare the log-agent native sidecar here
  containers:
    # TODO: declare the myapp main container here
  volumes:
    # TODO: declare the shared-logs emptyDir volume here
tasks.solution:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: native-sidecar-pod
spec:
  initContainers:
    # restartPolicy: Always transforms this init container into a native sidecar:
    # Kubernetes starts it before myapp but does NOT wait for it to exit.
    # It keeps running alongside myapp for the entire Pod lifetime,
    # and is restarted automatically if it crashes.
    - name: log-agent
      image: busybox:1.36
      restartPolicy: Always
      command: ['sh', '-c', 'tail -F /var/log/app/app.log']
      volumeMounts:
        - name: shared-logs
          mountPath: /var/log/app
  containers:
    - name: myapp
      image: busybox:1.36
      command:
        - sh
        - -c
        - "while true; do echo \"$(date) - request handled\" >> /var/log/app/app.log; sleep 3; done"
      volumeMounts:
        - name: shared-logs
          mountPath: /var/log/app
  volumes:
    - name: shared-logs
      emptyDir: {}
tasks.explanation: The single field `restartPolicy: Always` on an init container is what Kubernetes 1.28+ uses to distinguish a native sidecar from a normal init container. Without it, Kubernetes would wait for the container to exit before starting the next init container or the app. With it, Kubernetes considers the init phase complete as soon as the container has started (not finished), then immediately proceeds to start `myapp`. The sidecar then runs for the entire life of the Pod, is automatically restarted on crash, and receives a graceful termination signal only after `myapp` exits â€” enabling clean log flushing. The `emptyDir` volume is the communication channel: it exists in memory on the node, is shared by any container in the Pod that mounts it, and is destroyed when the Pod is deleted. `tail -F` (uppercase F) re-opens the file if it is rotated or does not yet exist, making it robust to the race where the sidecar starts before `myapp` has written the first line.
hints: Place `log-agent` under `initContainers:`, not under `containers:`. Adding `restartPolicy: Always` to an entry in `containers:` has no special effect â€” the native sidecar mechanism only works under `initContainers:`.

Both `log-agent` and `myapp` must reference the same volume name (`shared-logs`) in their `volumeMounts`. The `mountPath` can be the same path on both containers because each container has its own filesystem namespace; the volume is the bridge between them.

After applying the manifest, run `kubectl logs native-sidecar-pod -c log-agent` to confirm the sidecar is tailing the log file that `myapp` writes. If you see timestamped lines appearing every ~3 seconds, the shared-volume communication is working.

tasks.prompt:
Applied challenge â€” Config preparation + native sidecar in one Pod: Combine both patterns you have learned. Write a Pod YAML named `full-pattern-pod` that does the following in the correct lifecycle order:
1. **Init container** `prepare-config` (image `busybox:1.36`): writes the string `server_mode=production` to `/etc/app/config.ini` on a shared volume named `config-vol` (type `emptyDir`), then exits.
2. **Native sidecar** `config-watcher` (image `busybox:1.36`, `restartPolicy: Always`): runs `cat /etc/app/config.ini` every 10 seconds in an infinite loop, simulating a config-sync sidecar. Mount `config-vol` at `/etc/app`.
3. **Main container** `app` (image `nginx:1.27`, port 80): mounts `config-vol` at `/etc/app` so it can read the config file written by the init container.
Self-check: in your answer, state the exact startup order Kubernetes will use for these three containers and why.
tasks.starter:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: full-pattern-pod
spec:
  # HINT: initContainers must contain BOTH prepare-config AND config-watcher.
  # Their order in the list determines execution order.
  initContainers:
    # TODO
  containers:
    # TODO
  volumes:
    # TODO
tasks.solution:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: full-pattern-pod
spec:
  initContainers:
    # Step 1: classic init container â€” runs to completion before anything else.
    - name: prepare-config
      image: busybox:1.36
      command:
        - sh
        - -c
        - "mkdir -p /etc/app && echo 'server_mode=production' > /etc/app/config.ini && echo 'config written'"
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-vol
          mountPath: /etc/app
    # Step 2: native sidecar â€” starts after prepare-config exits,
    # but Kubernetes does NOT wait for it to finish before starting the app.
    - name: config-watcher
      image: busybox:1.36
      restartPolicy: Always
      command:
        - sh
        - -c
        - "while true; do echo '[config-watcher]'; cat /etc/app/config.ini; sleep 10; done"
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-vol
          mountPath: /etc/app
  containers:
    # Step 3: main app â€” starts after both init entries are satisfied.
    - name: app
      image: nginx:1.27
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-vol
          mountPath: /etc/app
  volumes:
    - name: config-vol
      emptyDir: {}

# STARTUP ORDER EXPLANATION (required by the prompt):
# 1. prepare-config starts and runs to completion (exit 0). No other container starts yet.
# 2. config-watcher starts. Because restartPolicy: Always marks it as a native sidecar,
#    Kubernetes does NOT wait for it to finish; it only waits until it is running.
# 3. app starts. It finds /etc/app/config.ini already populated by prepare-config.
tasks.explanation: This exercise cements the distinction between the two kinds of entries under `initContainers`. A plain init container (`prepare-config`) must run to completion before the next entry begins. A native sidecar (`config-watcher`, marked by `restartPolicy: Always`) is waited on only for startup â€” not for completion â€” so Kubernetes advances to the app container once the sidecar process is alive. The result is a three-phase lifecycle: (1) classic setup, (2) sidecar starts and stays running, (3) app starts and can read the prepared config. All three containers share `config-vol` via `emptyDir`. The init container writes the file, the sidecar and the app both read it. If `prepare-config` fails (for example, the volume is not mounted correctly), `config-watcher` and `app` will never start, which is exactly the safety guarantee you want: do not run the application if the configuration was not successfully prepared.
hints: Both `prepare-config` and `config-watcher` go under `initContainers:`. The list order is crucial: `prepare-config` must be listed first so it runs and writes the file before `config-watcher` tries to read it.

If `prepare-config` fails to create `/etc/app/config.ini` (for example, because the directory does not exist), add `mkdir -p /etc/app` before the `echo` command. The `emptyDir` volume is mounted as an empty directory, so the file must be created explicitly.

To verify the full sequence on a real cluster: `kubectl apply -f full-pattern-pod.yaml`, then run `kubectl logs full-pattern-pod -c prepare-config` (should show 'config written'), `kubectl logs full-pattern-pod -c config-watcher` (should show the config.ini contents every 10 seconds), and `kubectl exec full-pattern-pod -c app -- cat /etc/app/config.ini` (should print 'server_mode=production').
